item.title: Anthropic Rushes to Limit the Leak of Claude Code Source Code
item.description: Anthropic PBC is rushing to address the inadvertent release of internal source code behind Claude Code, an AI-powered assistant that has become a key moneymaker for the company.

item.title: Boeing Shares Soar On US Deal To Triple Patriot Seeker Output
item.description: Boeing Co. shares rose as much as 5.6% after the Pentagon announced a framework deal with the company to triple production of a crucial Patriot missile component over seven years.

item.title: Half of South Africa Wastewater Plants Deemed in Critical State
item.description: Almost half of South Africa’s wastewater facilities are in a critical state, raising the risk of them discharging untreated water into rivers and the environment.

item.title: Iranian Regime’s Grip on Local Internet Unshaken by Bombs
item.description: The Iranian regime is not losing its grip over the digital sphere.

item.title: India, Taiwan ETFs See Record Exodus Before Asia Stock Rebound
item.description: The largest US-listed exchange-traded funds tracking single Asian countries from India to Taiwan suffered record outflows in March, just ahead of a massive rebound in the region’s equities on the first day of April.

item.title: US Targets Canada’s Cloud-Computing Move as Trade Irritant
item.description: The US Trade Representative has again cited Canada’s laws about online platforms as trade barriers, and added the country’s sovereign-computing initiative to the list — a cluster of digital-economy issues that are likely to loom large in upcoming trade talks.

item.title: Traders Pour $977 Million Into Levered Bet That Oil Will Plunge
item.description: A cohort of oil traders have made a big leveraged bet that crude prices will tumble from their war-driven highs. So far, many are getting crushed.

item.title: Pfizer Wins €1.9 Billion Covid Vaccine Case With Poland, Romania
item.description: Poland and Romania have been ordered by a Belgian court to pay Pfizer Inc. €1.9 billion ($2.2 billion) for Covid-19 vaccines the two countries refused to take delivery of during the pandemic, handing a win to the drug maker’s efforts to enforce the contract.

item.title: SpaceX Has Filed Confidentially for IPO Ahead of AI Rivals
item.description: SpaceX has filed confidentially for an initial public offering, according to people familiar with the matter, bringing billionaire Elon Musk’s rocket, satellite and AI company closer to delivering the biggest-ever listing.

item.title: Gas-Turbine Prices Surge, Crimping Efforts to Power Data Centers
item.description: Surging demand for electricity is driving up prices for natural gas turbines, complicating efforts to supply power to a wave of data centers needed to support artificial intelligence.

item.title: TikTok Abandons Plans to Add Second Data Center in Ireland
item.description: ByteDance Ltd.’s TikTok has dropped plans to invest in a new Irish data center in a blow to the country’s reputation as a data center hub as it grapples with concerns over grid capacity.

item.title: India’s Central Bank Tightens Non-Deliverable FX Contract Rules
item.description: India’s monetary authority stepped up efforts to curb speculation against the battered rupee, restricting lenders from offering certain offshore foreign exchange derivative contracts, just days after it clamped down on banks’ local currency limits.

item.title: Raízen Proposes Creditors Take Large Equity Stake in Restructure
item.description: Brazil’s Raízen SA has presented creditors with details of its plan to restructure $12.6 billion of debt, which includes a grace period of at least five years and would see them take over a large equity stake and have more say in the sugar and ethanol producer, according to people familiar with the matter.

item.title: Top Gulf Aluminum Producer Halted Smelter After Iran Strike
item.description: Emirates Global Aluminium, the Middle East’s top producer of the metal, halted operations at its Al Taweelah smelter after the site was struck by Iranian missiles and drones over the weekend, according to a person familiar with the matter.

item.title: Shares of Garage Clothing’s Owner Jump on Strong Earnings Beat
item.description: Canadian fast-fashion retailer Groupe Dynamite Inc. reported earnings above estimates in its fourth quarter and provided an optimistic outlook for the current fiscal year. The shares rose.

item.title: OpenAI Demand Sinks on Secondary Market as Anthropic Runs Hot
item.description: OpenAI shares have fallen out of favor on the secondary market — in some cases becoming almost impossible to unload — as investors pivot quickly to Anthropic, its biggest competitor.

item.title: Dangote Refinery to Get More Nigerian Crude to Ease Local Supply
item.description: Nigeria is set to increase the number of crude cargoes allocated to the Dangote Refinery in a bid to boost domestic supply of refined products as the Iran war chokes traditional global energy routes.

item.title: Peru’s Inflation Tops Target in March for First Time in Two Years
item.description: Inflation in Peru’s capital city jumped the most in 32 years in March due to the combination of a domestic gas crisis, higher global oil prices and bad weather.

item.title: Colombia to Raise Gasoline Prices as Oil Costs Pressure Budget
item.description: Colombia will raise domestic gasoline prices this month as higher oil costs strain an already widening budget deficit.

item.title: BOE’s Gilt Repo Reform Plan Gets Pushback on Clearing, Haircuts
item.description: Bank of England plans to make the UK bond market more resilient in times of stress may carry risks, according to market participants.

item.title: Dubai Jet Billionaire Says Oil Spikes Risk Bankrupting Airlines
item.description: For Dubai-based billionaire Gediminas Ziemelis the aviation industry is experiencing a sense of deja-vu amid the Middle East conflict: grounded planes, plummeting demand and no clear sense of when it might all be over.

item.title: US Manufacturing Expands Most Since 2022, Input Costs Jump
item.description: US manufacturing activity expanded in March by the most since 2022, while input prices continued to surge amid the war with Iran.

item.title: Fed’s Musalem Says Current Rate Likely Appropriate for Some Time
item.description: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis President Alberto Musalem said risks are rising to both inflation and employment, and officials should be prepared to adjust interest rates in either direction depending on how the economy evolves.

item.title: Iran War Fans Steep Fuel Hikes in Africa, Presidential Pay Cut
item.description: With fuel prices surging as much as 81%, African governments are rolling out subsidies and cutting taxes to help cushion households from an increase in energy costs triggered by the US-Israeli war on Iran.

item.title: Intel to Pay $14 Billion to Buy Back Apollo Stake in Ireland Plant
item.description: Intel Corp., the chipmaker aiming to restore its manufacturing prowess, agreed to pay $14.2 billion to buy back half of a plant in Ireland that it had previously sold to Apollo Global Management.

item.title: BOE’s Bailey Says Traders Ahead of Themselves on Rate Hike Bets
item.description: Financial markets are “getting ahead of themselves” by pricing in interest-rate rises to contain inflation, according to Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey.

item.title: The Hottest Premium Credit Card Perk Is Wearable Fitness Trackers
item.description: As credit card companies with lofty fees compete for affluent consumers, the hottest new perks are wearable devices for tracking health, fitness and sleep.

item.title: AI Puts UK’s High-Paid Jobs and Tax Revenues at Risk, Study Says
item.description: Artificial intelligence threatens to leave many of the UK’s best-paid workers without a job — and the government without a crucial source of tax revenue, according to a new report.

item.title: Viktor Shvets on Castles without Moats and lots of Sharks
item.description: How to invest in an era of zero cost marginal costs.

item.title: US Retail Sales Rise by More Than Forecast in Broad Advance
item.description: US retail sales rebounded by more than forecast in February in a broad advance as consumers stepped up spending after a slow start to the year.
